text: The thoracoscope provides a view of the mediastinal pleura being opened to expose the azygos vein. The surgeon carefully dissects around the vein, encircles it, and ligates it below where it enters the superior vena cava (SVC). The vein is clipped above and divided, enhancing the exposure of the esophagus.

The surgeon identifies the bronchial artery behind the azygos vein, arising from the intercostal artery. The mediastinal pleura is incised anterior to the azygos vein, revealing direct esophageal branches from the descending thoracic aorta, which are sharply dissected. The dissection progresses from above to below towards the carina and then to the hiatus, with pneumo creating a plane by separating loose areolar structures. The thoracic duct is visible as a yellow structure behind the azygos vein and above the esophagus.

The surgeon carefully avoids opening the left pleura while staying close to the esophagus. The mediastinal pleura is incised anterior to the esophagus from the carina to the crura. The inferior pulmonary ligament is divided between the right lung and the esophagus up to the level of the inferior pulmonary vein. The right vagus nerve, visible as a white structure, is preserved to avoid division before branching to the right bronchus. A plane is created circumferentially around the esophagus, allowing the use of umbilical tape for traction to aid further dissection.

The surgeon uses a slightly tight umbilical tape tied around the esophagus to provide traction for dissection. The descending thoracic aorta is visible, with its branches to the esophagus being carefully taken down using harmonic or vessel sealers. The bulging structure above is the descending thoracic aorta, and posteriorly, the level of the esophageal growth is visible. Below the growth, the GE junction pad of fat and the circular brown diaphragmatic crura are being dissected.

The surgeon carefully dissects the esophagus away from the left bronchus and trachea, avoiding injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve by using a Ligasure device instead of cautery. The bronchial artery arising from the intercostal artery is taken down for better exposure. Loose fibers between the trachea and esophagus are sharply dissected. The dissection progresses above the left bronchus towards the thoracic inlet, marked by the inferior thyroid artery. Subcarinal lymphadenectomy is performed, preserving small arterial tributaries using a vessel sealer. The vagus nerve is gently pushed aside to preserve its branch to the left bronchus, completing the lymphadenectomy.

In the supracarinal region, the surgeon carefully dissects around the aortopulmonary window, preserving branches to the trachea from the aortic arch and descending aorta. The arch of the aorta is visible above, while the superior pulmonary vein is seen below. The left recurrent laryngeal nerve branches to the esophagus are sharply divided and pushed away. The inferior thyroid artery is identified, and lymph nodes along this artery are excised. The left vagus nerve is seen hooking around the arch of the aorta, giving rise to the left recurrent laryngeal nerve.

The surgeon focuses on the right side of the trachea, dissecting the mediastinal pleura away from the right vagus nerve. The right paratracheal lymph nodes are carefully dissected using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, including short bursts of vessel sealer. The superior vena cava is visible below, and the mediastinal pleura is detached from the superior part of the esophagus. The right recurrent laryngeal nerve, running parallel to the trachea, is identified and its branches to the esophagus are divided. The right vagus and recurrent laryngeal nerve are cleared of lymphofatty tissues, completing the right-sided paratracheal lymphadenectomy. The right subclavian artery is visible, and the recurrent laryngeal nerve is traced from the right vagus. An ICD is introduced, and the lung is inflated under vision. Laparoscopy is performed in the French position, using standard upper abdomen ports. The left lobe of the liver is retracted, and the gastrocolic ligament is dissected from the arcade, progressing from the right gastroepiploic artery to the fundus.

The surgeon performs dissection near the spleen, carefully releasing the short gastric vessels to avoid injury to the stomach. The fundus is separated from diaphragmatic attachments and brought down, revealing the esophagus being pulled down. Superior dissection of the esophagus is done, separating it from the crura and incising the phrenoesophageal membrane. The mediastinum is visible, and the tumor with crural involvement is taken down. Attention shifts to the right side, where the right crura is separated from the esophagus, completing circumferential dissection of the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon identifies the vertical structure as the right gastroepiploic artery, which is preserved. The omental attachments between the duodenum and colon are dissected, facilitating the movement of the gastric conduit towards the midline. The right gastrointestinal arcade is taken down, followed by the ligation of the left gastric artery. The gastric conduit is then prepared using an open technique.
